schools.summary: God himself fixing the borders of the promised land — 'the LORD spake unto Moses'; the word coming when God was ordering Israel, once over Jordan, to drive out the inhabitants and divide the land (Gill); God directing Moses to settle the line not as a geographer to please the curious, but as a prince in his grant, conveying a sure title, the description a deed of possession (Henry); God undertaking the office of a prudent careful father, fixing the boundaries lest the people's right to possess be ever called in question, the bounds making the title secure (Calvin); the inheritance no uncertain or disputed thing but a land measured and granted by the LORD, promised to Abraham long before (Gen 15:18); the God who appoints to all nations the bounds of their habitation (Acts 17:26) marking the portion of his own; the grant resting on God's word, not Israel's sword.

The land of Canaan given as an inheritance — 'when ye come into the land of Canaan; this is the land that shall fall unto you for an inheritance... with the coasts thereof'; the land spoken of as certainly to be possessed, the inheritance that should 'fall' to them by lot from God's hand; the fixing of the bounds God's own act, for it is he who determined the times and the bounds of men's habitations (Acts 17:26), the borders set in heaven before possessed on earth (Gill); the coasts particularly described to direct and bound Israel in their wars, that they not, like other empires, seek boundless enlargement but rest content with the land God gave, the bounds a curb on ambition (Wesley); the exact line known only by survey or a good map (Clarke); the typology — as Israel a bounded inheritance, so God's people a better abiding inheritance, the true Canaan (Heb 11:16).

themes: shall fall unto you for an inheritance

schools.summary: The south border — 'your south quarter shall be from the wilderness of Zin... the outmost coast of the salt sea eastward'; the south border running from east to west, beginning at the southern end of the Salt Sea (the Dead Sea) and along the edge of the wilderness of Zin and the border of Edom (Gill); the sea the Dead Sea, the lake Asphaltites, called the 'salt sea' from the salt and sulphurous taste of its waters, its eastern shore the corner whence the line set out (Clarke, Wesley); the border described from east to west by various windings, following the lie of the mountains and rivers, not a straight ruled line but the natural features; God beginning the bounding at the south-east corner and carrying the line westward; God marking every turn that his people might know precisely the land he gave, leaving nothing to dispute.

The turnings of the south border — 'your border shall turn from the south to the ascent of Akrabbim... to Kadeshbarnea... to Hazaraddar, and pass on to Azmon'; better 'to the south side of the ascent of Akrabbim' (the scorpion-pass, a rugged ascent up from the Arabah), the line turning there and passing to Zin, the goings forth south of Kadesh-barnea (Ellicott); Kadesh-barnea, the place of Israel's long sojourn and the sending of the spies, lying near the border, the line running south of it and on by Hazar-addar to Azmon, at the west end of the mountain of Edom (Gill, Wesley); the exact spots of some names no longer certain, yet a known and definite line of passes, towns, and wilderness; the south border skirting Edom and the southern desert, marking off Canaan from the wastes below; God leaving his people in no doubt of the extent of their grant.

The south border ending at the river of Egypt and the sea — 'the border shall fetch a compass from Azmon unto the river of Egypt, and the goings out of it shall be at the sea'; the line not straight but turning about, sweeping from Azmon to the brook of Egypt (Gill); that river — the eastern branch of the Nile or a river south of the Philistines (Clarke), or Sihor, the brook of Egypt that divided Egypt from Canaan (Josh 13:3), the wady commonly held to be el-Arish not the Nile itself (Wesley); 'the goings out at the sea' — the border running westward to the Great Sea (the Mediterranean), the midland sea, 'the sea' emphatically, the other 'seas' of the land but lakes (Wesley); the south-western corner fixed where the brook of Egypt met the sea, marking the land off from Egypt and the desert; the promised land reaching from the brook of Egypt to the great sea, a well-bounded inheritance.

themes: the goings out at the sea
from the brook of Egypt to the sea

schools.summary: The west border, the Great Sea — 'ye shall even have the great sea for a border: this shall be your west border'; unlike the winding south and north lines, the western boundary needing no tracing of points, the sea itself the border, the whole western coast washed by the Mediterranean; called the Great Sea or midland sea to distinguish it from the lesser waters — the Dead Sea, the Sea of Tiberias — which though called 'seas' were but lakes (Clarke, Wesley); 'the great sea and its coast' (Josh 15:47, Ellicott); the sea running along the whole western side, from the brook of Egypt in the south to the region of Lebanon, parallel with mount Lebanon (Wesley); God giving his people a sea-coast for their western frontier, a clear unmistakable bound; the great sea, fixed and unchanging, marking the western edge beyond all dispute.

themes: the great sea for a border
the midland sea, not a lake

schools.summary: The north border and its Mount Hor — 'this shall be your north border: from the great sea ye shall point out for you mount Hor'; the line drawn from the Mediterranean coast inland to a mountain called Hor; not the Hor where Aaron died (Num 20:28), which lay far south by Edom, but another mountain in the north, thought to be part of the range of Lebanon, elsewhere named as the northern border (Wesley, Gill); the northern boundary not determinable with certainty, the ancient names obscure (Keil and Delitzsch), not Hermon (too far eastward to suit the line) but some other height of Lebanon (Ellicott); the north border running from the sea-coast eastward along the Lebanon, marking off Canaan from Syria; though the exact peak unknown, the line plain to Israel and fixed by God's appointment; the northern frontier reaching up to the great mountains.

themes: from the great sea point out mount Hor
not the Hor where Aaron died

schools.summary: The north border by the entrance of Hamath — 'from mount Hor ye shall point out your border unto the entrance of Hamath; and the goings forth of the border shall be to Zedad'; the line running from the mountain to the 'entering in of Hamath,' a noted point often named as the northern limit (Josh 13:5); the 'entrance of Hamath' a narrow pass leading out of Canaan northward, by the great city of Hamath on the Orontes, the gateway between the Lebanon ranges through which the road to the north ran (Gill); the line passing on to Zedad and (next verse) Ziphron and Hazar-enan, connected with the like description in Ezek 47:16, probably crossing the northern Lebanon (Ellicott); several of these places now unknown, the names perished, yet the general line by the entrance of Hamath well marked; the land extending to the very threshold of the nations.

The end of the north border at Hazar-enan — 'the border shall go on to Ziphron, and the goings out of it shall be at Hazar-enan: this shall be your north border'; the line passing from Zedad to Ziphron and ending at Hazar-enan, the 'village of springs,' marking the north-eastern corner whence the east border would descend; Ziphron called Zapherin in the Jerusalem Targum, and Jerome thinking it a city Zephyrium in Cilicia, though that lies too far off to suit the border of Canaan, the exact place no longer sure (Gill); the names ancient and many now lost, yet the line clear in its course — from the great sea, by Mount Hor and the entrance of Hamath, to Hazar-enan; Hazar-enan the turning-point where the north border ended and the east began, the corner toward Damascus; God leaving no side of the grant unbounded.

themes: the border shall go on to Ziphron
this shall be your north border

schools.summary: The beginning of the east border — 'ye shall point out your east border from Hazar-enan to Shepham'; the eastern line setting out from the same Hazar-enan that closed the north border, running southward to Shepham; the place of the corner called Apamia by the Hebrews (after Jerome), and Shepham a city lying between Hazar-enan and Riblah, the next point (Gill); the east border, like the others, traced by named places point to point, though several now hard to fix; having gone up the south, west, and north, the line now coming down the east to enclose the whole, running southward by Shepham and Riblah down to the Sea of Galilee and the Jordan and the Salt Sea; this eastern line marking off Canaan proper, west of the Jordan, from the lands beyond, the inheritance of the two and a half tribes on the east side; God drawing the border all the way round, that the land might be wholly enclosed and known.

themes: the line comes down the east

schools.summary: The east border by the Sea of Galilee — 'the coast shall go down from Shepham to Riblah, on the east side of Ain... unto the side of the sea of Chinnereth eastward'; the line running down from Shepham to Riblah, set on the east side of Ain (a fountain), this Riblah distinct from the famous Riblah in the land of Hamath where the kings of Babylon judged (2 Kgs 25:6), Ain supposed the great spring at the foot of Antilibanus (Ellicott); Riblah held by some to be Daphne, near Antioch (Gill); the Sea of Chinnereth the same lake later called the Sea of Galilee, of Tiberias, and of Gennesaret (Clarke), the border running down to its eastern shore; from the high northern country the line coming down the east by Riblah and Ain to the freshwater sea of Galilee, and thence along the Jordan; God marking the eastern frontier by the very waters his people would later know.

themes: on the east side of Ain
the line by the Sea of Galilee

schools.summary: The east border ending at the Jordan and the Salt Sea, the circuit complete — 'the border shall go down to Jordan, and the goings out of it shall be at the salt sea: this shall be your land with the coasts thereof round about'; from the Sea of Galilee the line running down the Jordan to the Salt Sea, where the south border had begun, the circuit closed at the same south-eastern corner; the Jordan, renowned in both Testaments, rising at the foot of Lebanon, passing through Chinnereth, and emptying into the lake Asphaltites, the river the eastern bound of Canaan proper (Clarke); 'this shall be your land with the coasts thereof round about' — the four borders now drawn, the land bounded on every side, the grant complete and entire; God compassing the inheritance about, that his people possess a land whose every side was fixed by his word.

themes: the border shall go down to Jordan
the goings out at the salt sea
your land with the coasts round about

schools.summary: The land divided by lot among the nine tribes and a half — 'this is the land which ye shall inherit by lot... to give unto the nine tribes, and to the half tribe'; the bounded land apportioned not by the people's choice or strength but by lot, each tribe receiving its share as from the hand of God; the land emphatically described as of many advantages — climate, soil, situation — a goodly inheritance (Clarke); the command, though a repetition, not superfluous, contrasting the new allotment of the nine and a half tribes west of Jordan with the former grant of Bashan and Gilead already given to the two and a half tribes east of Jordan (Calvin); the dividing by lot, under God's overruling, preventing all envy and contention, each receiving what God assigned (Prov 16:33); the typology — as each tribe its lot in Canaan, so the saints their inheritance assigned by God (Col 1:12).

themes: the nine tribes and the half tribe

schools.summary: The two and a half tribes already provided — 'the tribe of the children of Reuben... the tribe of the children of Gad... and half the tribe of Manasseh, have received their inheritance'; these, with their families, having received their portion already on the east side of Jordan; when Israel came to the rich pasture-lands of Gilead and Bashan, conquered from Sihon and Og, Reuben and Gad, having much cattle, asked for that territory, and half Manasseh with them, Moses granting it on condition they go over armed to help win Canaan (Num 32:33); having their portion settled east of Jordan, these tribes not to share in the lot of the land west of it, the dividing by lot for the nine and a half who remained unprovided; God so arranging that every tribe should have its inheritance, none left without a portion.

The eastern portion confirmed — 'the two tribes and the half tribe have received their inheritance on this side Jordan near Jericho eastward, toward the sunrising'; their inheritance across the Jordan from Canaan proper, on its eastern bank, over against Jericho, toward the rising sun, 'along the side or bank of Jordan,' stretching along the river's eastern side (Num 32:19, Ellicott); the tribes Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh, whose portion distinct from and outside the bounded land of Canaan (Gill); the land west of Jordan, marked by the four borders, the proper inheritance to be divided by lot, while the eastern tribes' portion lay beyond that border, granted earlier and on other terms; this making clear why the lot of Canaan was for nine tribes and a half only; God providing for the whole people, some east and some west, none without an inheritance.

themes: on this side Jordan near Jericho

schools.summary: God appointing the men to divide the land — 'the LORD spake unto Moses'; God naming commissioners for the dividing while the conquest is taken for granted, though not a stroke yet struck, and naming not the generals who should fight but the men who should parcel it out, so sure was the victory in God's purpose that he ordered the division before the battle (Henry); the division to be made impartially and to the satisfaction of all the tribes, by men of God's own choosing (Gill); if the land was divided by lot, why were judges needed — the lot determining the place of each tribe, but the appointed men needed to order the business, measure the portions to the size of each tribe, and give the lot its due authority (Calvin); God speaking of dividing a land not yet entered as a thing certain, his people reckoning the promise as good as the possession.

Eleazar and Joshua set over the division — 'these are the names of the men which shall divide the land unto you: Eleazar the priest, and Joshua the son of Nun'; these to 'divide' or better 'allot' the land as an inheritance, to take possession in the people's name and stead, as their representatives, and distribute it among the tribes (Gill, Ellicott); the fitness of the two — Eleazar the high priest and Joshua, the spiritual head and the civil-military head, joined over the work, the dividing both a sacred and a public act; Eleazar to act in God's name, to cast the lots, to prevent contention, to consult God by the Urim in any difficulty, to transact the whole in a solemn and religious manner, the lot drawn under God's eye (Wesley); the honour on Joshua, who having led the people into the land should also divide it; so weighty a matter committed to men of God's naming.

themes: priest and leader head the work
the lot cast in God's name

schools.summary: A prince of each tribe joined to the work — 'ye shall take one prince of every tribe, to divide the land by inheritance'; besides the high priest and the leader, a chief man or prince chosen from each of the tribes that were to receive a portion, one from each of the ten tribes interested (the nine and a half west of Jordan, Ellicott); none from Reuben or Gad, who had already received their inheritance east of Jordan and had no part in this division (Gill); each tribe having its own prince among the dividers, so no tribe might suspect itself wronged or another favoured, the presence of a representative from every interested tribe securing impartiality and acceptance; the joining of authorities — the priest (sacred), the leader (public), and the princes (tribal) together, the parting of the inheritance done with the consent and oversight of all; God providing for the satisfaction of the people, every tribe owning a hand in the work.

themes: to divide the land by inheritance

schools.summary: The naming of the princes, beginning with Caleb of Judah — 'the names of the men are these: Of the tribe of Judah, Caleb the son of Jephunneh'; the men not left to the tribes to elect but named by the LORD himself, who best knew their fitness, the dividers of God's appointment man by man (Gill); Caleb, the faithful spy who with Joshua brought back a good report and wholly followed the LORD (Num 14:24), set at the head of the princes, of the tribe that led the camp, the man who believed God concerning the land honoured to help divide it; Moses not following the order of the tribes used in chs 1, 7, 26 but ranging them otherwise, the order conformed to the order of their inheritances as these afterward fell by lot, the list written before the lot agreeing with the lot God would give — a demonstration of the infinite foreknowledge of God (Clarke, Wesley).

themes: the names of the men are these

schools.summary: The dividers commissioned by the LORD — 'these are they whom the LORD commanded to divide the inheritance unto the children of Israel in the land of Canaan'; God not only naming and appointing these men (Eleazar, Joshua, and the twelve princes) but laying his express command upon them, binding and obliging them, the dividing theirs by divine charge not human arrangement (Gill); the whole order — the bounds of the land, the men to divide it, their names and ranks — made and recorded before the land was conquered, before Israel had crossed the Jordan or struck a blow, God providing for the parting of the inheritance while it was yet in enemy hands, so sure the promise; the inheritance as good as theirs already, the dividers named, the work charged, faith counting the land won before the war began; God ordering the future of his people with a sure hand.
